title: Kelly Criterion & Bet Sizing
subtitle: Growth-optimal fractions, log-utility, half-Kelly, and the cost of over-betting
intro: Knowing a bet is +EV is only half the job; the other half is sizing it. Bet too little and you leave growth on the table; bet too much and variance — or ruin — eats you alive. The Kelly criterion answers 'what fraction of bankroll?' by maximizing the long-run growth rate of wealth, and it is a staple of trading-desk and poker interviews for exactly that reason.

The key reframing: you do not maximize expected wealth (that recommends betting everything on any +EV edge and going broke the first time you lose). You maximize expected LOG wealth, because wealth compounds multiplicatively. The fraction that maximizes E[log(1 + f·outcome)] is the Kelly fraction, and it is what makes wealth grow fastest almost surely over many bets.

For an even-money bet the answer is gorgeous: f* = 2p − 1, i.e. bet your edge. For general odds b-to-1, f* = (bp − q)/b = edge ÷ odds. With no edge you bet nothing; with a large edge Kelly still keeps you well short of all-in.

Real desks bet fractional Kelly — often half-Kelly — because the growth curve is flat near the top while variance falls fast: half-Kelly keeps about three-quarters of the growth for half the volatility, and protects against the very real danger that your estimated edge is too high. Over-betting is punished sharply: at twice Kelly the long-run growth rate drops all the way back to zero. This lesson builds on expected value and logs (L1, L12).

primer.heading: Size by maximizing log-growth
body: Wealth compounds **multiplicatively**: after many bets your money is a product of growth factors, so the right objective is the **expected log return** per bet, not expected wealth.

Maximizing expected wealth says 'bet everything on any edge' — and one loss wipes you out. Maximizing $E[\log(\text{wealth})]$ instead gives the fraction that makes wealth grow fastest with probability 1 over the long run.

keyTerms.definition: The bankroll fraction that maximizes expected log growth per bet.
Scoring outcomes by `log(wealth)` because returns compound multiplicatively.

visual.expression: maximize  g(f) = E[ ln(1 + f·R) ]   (R = per-unit outcome)
visual.caption: Kelly maximizes the expected log growth rate, not expected wealth.

primer.heading: The even-money Kelly fraction
body: For a bet that pays **even money** (1:1) and wins with probability $p$ (so $q = 1-p$), the Kelly fraction is simply your **edge**: $f^* = p - q = 2p - 1$.
Win 55% of even-money bets? Bet 10% of bankroll. Win 60%? Bet 20%. No edge ($p=0.5$)? Bet nothing.

visual.caption: Bet your edge on an even-money proposition.

example.prompt: An even-money bet wins 60% of the time. Kelly fraction?
example.result: f* = 0.20 — risk 20% of bankroll per bet.

primer.heading: General odds: edge ÷ odds
body: When a winning bet pays $b$-to-1 (net), the Kelly fraction is $f^* = \dfrac{bp - q}{b} = p - \dfrac{q}{b}$. A handy reading: **edge divided by odds**, where edge is the EV per unit staked, $bp - q$.
This reduces to $2p-1$ when $b = 1$. Bigger payout odds let you size a given win-probability more aggressively per unit of edge.

visual.expression: f* = (b·p − q) / b = p − q/b     (b = net payout odds)
visual.caption: General Kelly fraction for a b-to-1 bet.

example.prompt: A bet pays 2-to-1 and wins with probability 0.4. Kelly fraction?

primer.heading: Fractional & half-Kelly
body: The growth curve $g(f)$ is **flat near its peak**, so betting a bit under Kelly costs almost no growth but slashes variance. Desks routinely bet **half-Kelly**.

Rule of thumb: half-Kelly retains about **three-quarters** of the maximum growth rate for roughly **half** the volatility — and it cushions the very real risk that your edge estimate is too optimistic (over-estimating $p$ pushes full Kelly into dangerous over-betting).

visual.title: Why pros bet fractional Kelly
visual.text: Near the top the growth curve barely changes, but variance keeps rising. Half-Kelly ≈ 75% of the growth at ≈ half the risk, and it hedges a mis-estimated edge.

primer.heading: Over-betting: the cost of greed
body: Betting **more** than Kelly *lowers* your long-run growth and amplifies drawdowns. The damage is asymmetric: under-betting costs a little, over-betting costs a lot.

The sharp landmark: betting exactly **twice** the Kelly fraction drives the long-run growth rate all the way back to **zero** — you take maximum risk for no compounding benefit. Beyond that, growth goes negative and ruin becomes the destination.

visual.expression: g(f*) = max ;   g(2f*) ≈ 0 ;   g(f) < 0 for f > 2f*
visual.caption: Double the Kelly fraction ⇒ zero long-run growth.

primerNarration.title: Maximize log, not dollars
primerNarration.caption: Wealth compounds multiplicatively, so the right thing to maximize is expected log growth, not expected wealth. Maximizing expected wealth tells you to bet everything on any edge, and a single loss wipes you out. Log utility gives the fraction that grows wealth fastest over the long run.

primerNarration.title: Bet your edge
primerNarration.caption: For an even-money bet, the Kelly fraction is just your edge: two p minus one. Win sixty percent of the time and you risk twenty percent of your bankroll. With no edge, you bet nothing.

primerNarration.title: Edge divided by odds
primerNarration.caption: When a win pays b to one, the Kelly fraction is b times p minus q, all over b — read it as edge divided by odds. With a payout of two to one and a forty percent win chance, that works out to ten percent of bankroll.

primerNarration.title: Most of the growth, half the risk
primerNarration.caption: Because the growth curve is flat near its peak, betting a little under Kelly barely costs growth but greatly reduces variance. Half-Kelly keeps about three quarters of the growth for roughly half the volatility, and it protects you when your estimated edge is too optimistic.

visual.title: Fractional Kelly
visual.text: Flat top, falling variance — bet under the peak.

primerNarration.title: Double Kelly kills growth
primerNarration.caption: Betting more than Kelly lowers your long-run growth and deepens drawdowns, and the penalty is steep. Bet exactly twice the Kelly fraction and your long-run growth rate falls all the way to zero — maximum risk for no compounding reward.

questions.question: An even-money (1:1) bet wins with probability 0.6. What fraction of bankroll does the Kelly criterion say to bet?

explanations.A: Correct. For even money, f* = 2p − 1 = 2·0.6 − 1 = 0.20.
explanations.B: 0.60 is the win probability p, not the Kelly fraction.
explanations.C: 0.40 is q-related (or 2·0.2) — it doubles the edge incorrectly; the edge itself is 0.2.
explanations.D: 0.10 is half-Kelly here, not the full Kelly fraction.

remediation.question: Even-money bet, win probability 0.55. Kelly fraction?

explanations.B: 0.55 is p, not the fraction.
explanations.C: 0.45 is q, the loss probability.
explanations.D: 0.20 doubles the correct edge.

remediation.question: Even-money bet, win probability 0.50. Kelly fraction?

explanations.A: Correct. 2·0.5 − 1 = 0; with no edge you bet nothing.
explanations.B: 0.5 is the win probability, not the fraction.
explanations.C: 1 (all-in) ignores that there is no edge to exploit.
explanations.D: 0.25 has no basis when the edge is 0.

questions.question: The Kelly criterion chooses the bet fraction that maximizes:
options: Expected logarithm of wealth (long-run growth rate)
The probability of winning the next bet

explanations.A: Correct. Because wealth compounds multiplicatively, maximizing E[log wealth] maximizes the long-run growth rate.
explanations.B: Maximizing one-step expected wealth says bet everything on any edge, which leads to ruin.
explanations.C: Kelly does not change your win probability; it sizes the bet given that probability.
explanations.D: Kelly does not maximize variance; if anything it controls it relative to growth.

remediation.question: Why maximize expected LOG wealth instead of expected wealth?
options: Wealth compounds multiplicatively, so log turns products into sums
Logs make the math harder on purpose
Expected wealth is impossible to compute

explanations.A: Correct. Multiplicative compounding means the long-run rate is the average of log growth factors.
explanations.B: Logs actually simplify multiplicative growth into additive terms.
explanations.C: Expected wealth is easy to compute; it just gives a reckless answer.
explanations.D: Logs do not eliminate losses; they optimize growth.

remediation.question: Maximizing one-step expected wealth on any +EV edge recommends:
options: Betting your entire bankroll, risking ruin

explanations.A: Correct. Linear expected wealth is maximized by going all-in on any positive edge — which busts you on the first loss.
explanations.B: Betting nothing would not maximize expected wealth on a +EV bet.
explanations.C: Half-Kelly comes from log utility, not from maximizing expected wealth.
explanations.D: The Kelly fraction comes from log utility, not from maximizing expected wealth.

questions.question: A bet pays 2-to-1 (win 2 units per 1 staked) and wins with probability 0.4. What is the Kelly fraction f* = (bp − q)/b?

explanations.B: 0.20 = bp − q = 0.2 is the edge (numerator); you must still divide by b = 2.
explanations.C: 0.40 is the win probability p, not the fraction.
explanations.D: 0.30 mishandles the arithmetic; the correct value is 0.10.

remediation.question: A bet pays 3-to-1 and wins with probability 0.5. Kelly fraction f* = (bp − q)/b?

explanations.B: 0.5 is p, not the fraction.
explanations.C: 0.25 ignores the 3-to-1 payout.
explanations.D: 1 (all-in) is far above Kelly.

remediation.question: The general Kelly fraction f* = (bp − q)/b reduces, for an even-money bet (b = 1), to:

explanations.A: Correct. With b = 1, (p − q)/1 = p − (1 − p) = 2p − 1.
explanations.B: p alone is the win probability, not the edge.
explanations.C: p − 1 is negative for p < 1 and is not the fraction.
explanations.D: 1 − p = q is the loss probability.

questions.question: On an even-money bet you win 55% of the time. What is your edge, and (for even money) the Kelly fraction?

explanations.A: Correct. Edge = p − q = 0.55 − 0.45 = 0.10, and for even money the Kelly fraction equals the edge, 0.10.
explanations.B: 0.55 is p, the win probability, not the edge.
explanations.C: 0.05 = p − 0.5 measures distance from 50% but the edge over an even-money opponent is p − q = 0.10.
explanations.D: 0.45 is q, the loss probability.

remediation.question: Even-money bet, p = 0.70. Edge?

explanations.D: 0.20 = p − 0.5 doubled-down incorrectly; edge is p − q = 0.40.

remediation.question: For an even-money bet, the Kelly fraction equals:

explanations.A: Correct. f* = 2p − 1 = p − q, exactly the edge.
explanations.B: p alone over-bets relative to Kelly.
explanations.C: Half of p has no basis.
explanations.D: All-in ignores the size of the edge.

questions.question: You face an even-money bet that you win exactly half the time (p = 0.5). What does Kelly recommend?
options: Bet a small fixed amount anyway

explanations.A: Correct. With no edge, f* = 2·0.5 − 1 = 0; risking capital on a zero-edge bet only adds variance.
explanations.B: Half the bankroll would be reckless with no edge.
explanations.C: All-in on a fair bet is pure gambling with no growth benefit.
explanations.D: Any positive stake on a zero-edge bet reduces long-run growth via variance.

remediation.question: An even-money bet you LOSE more than half the time (p < 0.5) gives a Kelly fraction that is:
options: Negative — i.e. don't take it (or bet the other side)

explanations.A: Correct. f* = 2p − 1 < 0 when p < 0.5, meaning the bet is unfavorable — decline it.
explanations.B: It is not positive when you lose more than half the time.
explanations.C: 0.5 has no basis here.
explanations.D: Betting all-in on a losing proposition guarantees rapid ruin.

remediation.question: Betting a positive fraction on a zero-edge even-money bet affects long-run growth how?
options: It lowers it (variance with no compensating drift)

explanations.A: Correct. With no edge, the variance drag on log-growth makes any stake harmful.
explanations.B: There is no edge to raise growth.
explanations.C: Variance still reduces the geometric growth rate.
explanations.D: A fair bet guarantees nothing.

questions.question: Compared with full Kelly, betting half-Kelly mainly does what?
options: Keeps most of the growth (~3/4) while roughly halving volatility
Is identical to full Kelly in every way

explanations.A: Correct. The growth curve is flat near its peak, so half-Kelly retains roughly three-quarters of the growth for about half the volatility — and hedges a mis-estimated edge.
explanations.B: Betting less cannot double the growth rate; full Kelly already maximizes it.
explanations.C: Half-Kelly still has losing streaks; it reduces, not eliminates, risk.
explanations.D: It deliberately trades a little growth for much less variance, so it is not identical.

remediation.question: The main practical reason desks bet fractional Kelly is:
options: Lower variance and protection against an over-estimated edge

explanations.A: Correct. Fractional Kelly cuts volatility sharply and guards against optimistic edge estimates.
explanations.B: Bet sizing does not change the underlying edge.
explanations.C: It is a risk choice, not a regulatory rule.
explanations.D: Maximizing one-step expected wealth would push toward all-in, the opposite.

remediation.question: If your full-Kelly fraction is 0.2, half-Kelly is:

explanations.A: Correct. Half of 0.2 is 0.1.
explanations.B: 0.4 is double Kelly, not half.
explanations.C: 0.2 is full Kelly.
explanations.D: 0.05 is quarter-Kelly.

questions.question: Betting MORE than the Kelly fraction (but not yet ruinous) does what to long-run growth?

explanations.A: Correct. Kelly is the unique growth-maximizer, so any deviation — especially over-betting — reduces growth and adds variance.
explanations.B: Over-betting cannot raise growth above the Kelly maximum.
explanations.C: Growth changes (falls) once you leave the optimal fraction.
explanations.D: Modest over-betting lowers growth gradually; immediate bankruptcy requires far more extreme sizing.

remediation.question: The penalty for over-betting vs under-betting by the same amount is:

explanations.A: Correct. The growth curve falls off faster above the peak, so over-betting is more damaging than equal under-betting.
explanations.B: The penalty is not symmetric around the peak.
explanations.C: Deviations from Kelly do reduce growth.
explanations.D: The asymmetry applies to any edge, not just negative ones.

remediation.question: Which sizing maximizes long-run growth for a +EV bet?

explanations.A: Correct. Kelly is by definition the growth-rate maximizer.
explanations.B: Double Kelly drops growth to zero.
explanations.C: All-in risks ruin.
explanations.D: Zero forgoes the edge entirely.

questions.question: Betting exactly TWICE the Kelly fraction gives a long-run growth rate of approximately:
options: A small positive bonus over Kelly

explanations.A: Correct. A classic Kelly result: at 2f* the long-run growth rate falls back to zero — maximum risk, no compounding gain.
explanations.B: Growth does not scale with bet size; doubling the fraction destroys growth, not doubles it.
explanations.C: The maximum growth is at f*, not 2f*.
explanations.D: There is no bonus; 2f* is the break-even point for growth.

remediation.question: At the Kelly fraction f*, the long-run growth rate is:

explanations.A: Correct. f* is defined as the growth-maximizing fraction.
explanations.B: Zero growth occurs at 2f*, not f*.
explanations.C: Growth is positive (and maximal) at f* for a +EV bet.
explanations.D: f* maximizes, not minimizes, growth.

remediation.question: Betting MORE than twice the Kelly fraction leads to a long-run growth rate that is:

explanations.A: Correct. Beyond 2f* growth turns negative, so wealth shrinks toward ruin over time.
explanations.B: Past the zero-growth point growth is negative, not positive.
explanations.C: The maximum is at f*, far below 2f*.
explanations.D: Growth is exactly zero only at 2f*; beyond it, growth is negative.

questions.question: A bet pays 3-to-1 (win 3 per 1 staked) and wins with probability 0.3. What is the Kelly fraction?

explanations.B: 0.20 = bp − q is the edge (numerator) before dividing by b = 3.
explanations.C: 0.30 is the win probability p.
explanations.D: 0.10 would be the edge divided by 2, but the odds here are 3.

remediation.question: A bet pays 4-to-1 and wins with probability 0.3. Kelly fraction (bp − q)/b?

explanations.B: 0.5 = bp − q is the edge before dividing by b = 4.
explanations.D: 0.2 uses the wrong odds.

remediation.question: In f* = (bp − q)/b, the numerator bp − q represents:
options: The expected profit (edge) per unit staked

explanations.A: Correct. bp − q is the EV per unit staked — the edge — which you then divide by the odds b.
explanations.B: The win probability is p, not bp − q.
explanations.C: The odds are b, the divisor.
explanations.D: The fraction is the whole expression, not just the numerator.

questions.question: You can win with probability 0.5 on a bet that pays 2-to-1. What is the Kelly fraction?

explanations.B: 0.50 is the win probability p.
explanations.C: 0.20 uses the wrong odds or arithmetic.
explanations.D: 0.10 under-sizes; the favorable 2-to-1 payout with a coin-flip win warrants 0.25.

remediation.question: Win probability 0.5, payout 5-to-1. Kelly fraction (bp − q)/b?

explanations.C: 0.20 mishandles the odds.
explanations.D: 0.10 under-sizes for such a favorable payout.

remediation.question: Holding win probability fixed, a larger payout b makes the Kelly fraction:
options: Larger (you can size up a given edge)

explanations.A: Correct. A bigger payout raises bp − q faster than the divisor b, increasing f* (up to a point), so a favorable price warrants more size.
explanations.B: It does not shrink the optimal fraction for a fixed favorable p.
explanations.C: The fraction does change with the odds.
explanations.D: For a +EV bet the fraction stays positive.

questions.question: For an even-money bet with p = 0.6 (Kelly fraction 0.2), the long-run growth rate per bet is g = 0.6·ln(1.2) + 0.4·ln(0.8). This is approximately:

explanations.B: ≈ 20% confuses the bet fraction (0.2) with the growth rate.
explanations.C: ≈ 10% confuses the edge (0.1) with the growth rate.
explanations.D: 0% would be the growth at twice-Kelly (f = 0.4), not at the optimal 0.2.

remediation.question: The Kelly growth rate g(f) for a bet with outcome R is:

explanations.A: Correct. The long-run growth rate is the expected log of the per-bet wealth multiplier.
explanations.B: That is the expected multiplier (linear), not the log-growth rate.
explanations.C: f·E[R] is the expected return, not the geometric growth rate.
explanations.D: ln(E[wealth]) is not the same as E[ln(wealth)] (Jensen's inequality).

remediation.question: The growth rate at the Kelly fraction is, compared with any other fraction:

explanations.A: Correct. By construction the Kelly fraction maximizes the growth rate.
explanations.B: It is the maximum, not the minimum.
explanations.C: It is positive for a +EV bet.
explanations.D: It is positive, not negative, for a genuine edge.

questions.question: Your Kelly fraction is 0.2 (even money, p = 0.6). If you instead bet 0.4 of bankroll each time (double Kelly), your long-run growth rate is approximately:
options: About zero — you tread water despite max risk
About double the Kelly growth rate
Higher, since you bet more on a +EV edge

explanations.A: Correct. g(0.4) = 0.6·ln(1.4) + 0.4·ln(0.6) ≈ 0.2019 − 0.2043 ≈ 0 — twice-Kelly drives growth to roughly zero.
explanations.B: Betting double does not double growth; it destroys it.
explanations.C: Over-betting a +EV edge lowers growth; more size is not always better.
explanations.D: ≈ 40% confuses the bet fraction with the growth rate; the rate is ≈ 0.

remediation.question: At what multiple of the Kelly fraction does the long-run growth rate return to zero?

explanations.A: Correct. Growth is zero at twice the Kelly fraction.
explanations.B: At 1× Kelly growth is maximal, not zero.
explanations.C: At half-Kelly growth is still strongly positive.
explanations.D: By 10× Kelly growth is deeply negative, well past zero.

remediation.question: Over-betting (above Kelly) tends to produce:

explanations.A: Correct. Past the peak, growth falls and volatility (drawdowns) rises.
explanations.B: Over-betting worsens both growth and drawdowns.
explanations.C: Both change for the worse once you exceed Kelly.
explanations.D: Nothing about over-betting guarantees profit.
